config.title: Doctor in Berlin - FAQ
config.subtitle: Short answers for tourists choosing between 112, 116117, doctors, pharmacies, hospitals and insurance.

items.q: How do I find a doctor in Berlin as a tourist?
items.a: For a stable problem, search for Allgemeinmedizin, Hausarzt, Praxis or MVZ near your hotel, ask your accommodation or insurer for help, and bring ID plus insurance details. For urgent non-emergency help, use 116117.

items.q: When should I call 112 in Berlin?
items.a: Call 112 for real emergencies: severe chest pain, serious breathing trouble, heavy bleeding, major accident, collapse, signs of stroke, severe allergic reaction or any situation where waiting feels unsafe.
116117 is the medical on-call service for urgent but non-life-threatening problems, especially when normal practices are closed or the issue cannot comfortably wait until the next working day.

items.q: Should I go to a hospital emergency department in Berlin?
items.a: Go to hospital or call 112 for serious or potentially serious cases. For stable problems, a normal doctor, 116117 or a pharmacy route is often calmer than going straight to an emergency department.

items.q: Can I use my European Health Insurance Card in Berlin?
items.a: EU visitors should carry their EHIC. It can help with medically necessary state-provided healthcare during a temporary stay, but it does not replace travel insurance or automatically cover private treatment.

items.q: Can a Berlin pharmacy help if I feel unwell?
items.a: Yes, for medicine advice, minor symptoms, over-the-counter remedies and prescription handling. If symptoms are severe, worsening or not really a pharmacy question, switch to 116117 or 112.
